title: The Development of British Democracy
overview: British democracy has grown over many centuries. It began with limits placed on royal power and developed through reforms that gave ordinary citizens a voice in government. Key moments such as the Magna Carta, the Bill of Rights, and the Reform Acts helped shape today's system of freedom, fairness and accountability.

study_sections.heading: Early Democracy
study_sections.content: Magna Carta (1215): Signed by King John; first limited monarch's power and guaranteed basic legal rights. Parliament developed during Middle Ages as nobles and commoners met to advise the king. Civil War (1642-1651): Conflict between King Charles I and Parliament; ended with Parliament's victory and principle that rulers must obey law.

study_sections.heading: Expanding Rights
study_sections.content: Bill of Rights (1689): Confirmed Parliament's powers, limited royal authority, established freedom of speech in Parliament. Reform Acts (1832, 1867, 1884): Expanded voting rights to more men and improved fair representation. Secret Ballot Act (1872): Allowed people to vote privately.

study_sections.heading: Women's Suffrage
study_sections.content: Women over 30 gained vote in 1918. Equal voting rights with men granted in 1928. Representation of the People Act (1969): Lowered voting age from 21 to 18.

study_sections.heading: Modern Democracy
study_sections.content: Universal suffrage: Today, all citizens aged 18 or over can vote in free elections. Devolution (late 20th century): Gave Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland their own assemblies or parliaments. Britain's democracy rests on values of fairness, accountability, and participation.

key_facts: British democracy evolved gradually through key events, documents, and institutions.
Magna Carta in 1215 forced King John to accept that the monarch is not above the law.
The Model Parliament of 1295 included representatives from counties and towns, signalling wider political participation.
Conflict between Crown and Parliament led to the English Civil War (1642–1651) and the Commonwealth under Oliver Cromwell.
The monarchy was restored in 1660 but with tighter parliamentary limits.
The Glorious Revolution of 1688 installed William III and Mary II and confirmed parliamentary supremacy.
The Bill of Rights 1689 required regular parliaments, free elections, and banned cruel punishments.
The Act of Union 1707 united England and Scotland with a single Parliament at Westminster.
Reform Acts in 1832, 1867, and 1884 extended the vote to a wider share of men and reformed electoral districts.
The Representation of the People Act 1918 enfranchised most men over 21 and women over 30 who met property rules.
The Equal Franchise Act 1928 granted equal voting rights to men and women at age 21, and the 1969 Act lowered the voting age to 18.
Political parties such as the Conservatives, Liberals, and Labour gave voters clear choices, supported by trade unions.
The House of Lords Act 1999 reduced hereditary peers, modernising the second chamber.
Today, free elections, independent courts, and free expression maintain the UK’s democratic system.
memory_hook: Picture democracy as an oak tree—rooted in Magna Carta, growing through reform acts, and branching into today’s universal vote.
